subject: Mathematics
contextNote: Sec 2 Math, 24 students. You modelled equivalent fractions using 3/4 = 9/12, showing the multiply-top-and-bottom-by-the-same-number rule explicitly on the board. Students are now 22 minutes into a set of equivalence questions. The readiness gap is visible — some students are flying, some are stuck on why the method works, and some have not started.

students.name: Rajan
students.note: Multiple equivalent pairs correct and fast. Has started generating his own examples beyond the set questions.

students.name: Nur Atiqah
students.note: Correct equivalent pairs for most questions. Has not written the common denominator step — just the final fraction.

students.name: Wei Jie
students.note: Written 2/5 = 4/10 — multiplied the numerator by 2 but not the denominator. Partial error, not random.

students.name: Siti
students.note: Page mostly blank. Has copied the question numbers but not attempted the fractions.

ep1.situation: 22 minutes into the lesson. You circulate and the readiness gap is stark. Rajan has completed the set questions correctly and is now generating his own equivalent fraction chains — 1/3 = 2/6 = 4/12 = 8/24 — working independently and with confidence. Nur has correct equivalent pairs for most questions but has written only the final fraction with no working — you cannot see whether she understands the multiply-both-by-the-same-number rule or is using a different method. Wei Jie has written 2/5 = 4/10 — he multiplied the numerator by 2 but left the denominator as 10 instead of also multiplying it. The error is consistent across two questions, suggesting a partial understanding of the rule. Siti's page is mostly blank — she has copied the question numbers but has not attempted any of the fractions. She is looking at her neighbour's page. A quick scan of the class: 8 students are working correctly and confidently like Rajan. 10 students have some correct answers but inconsistent working like Nur. 6 students have patterned errors like Wei Jie or blank pages like Siti. You have about 18 minutes of lesson time remaining.
ep1.evidence: The gap is visible in three tiers: a third are working correctly and independently, a third have correct answers but no visible working or reasoning, and a third have patterned errors or have not started. The most common error is multiplying only the numerator, not both numerator and denominator.

choices.label: Two-step equivalence diagnostic
choices.elaboration: Stop the class and project a diagnostic question: '3/6 = ?/12.' Ask two questions in sequence: '(1) What is the missing numerator?' Then: '(2) Why did you multiply by that number? Choose: (a) Because I multiplied top and bottom by the same number. (b) Because I found the common denominator. (c) Because I guessed. (d) I used a different method.' Ask students to write both answers on paper and hold them up. Use the pattern to see who understands the multiply-both rule and who is getting correct answers by a different method or by guessing.
choices.tradeoff: The two-step structure reveals both the answer and the reasoning, giving you whole-class evidence of where the understanding sits. But it halts the practice of students who are working productively, like Rajan. Students who are guessing correctly may struggle to articulate a method and feel exposed.

choices.label: Tiered visual scaffolds: strips, number lines, abstract
choices.elaboration: Distribute differentiated task cards without stopping the class. Struggling students like Siti receive fraction strips — physical or drawn representations where they can shade and compare fractions visually to find equivalents. Middle students like Nur and Wei Jie receive number line cards where they mark both fractions and check they land on the same point. Fast finishers like Rajan receive an abstract extension: 'Prove that 3/4 = 9/12 using two different methods. Which method is more efficient? Why?' All three versions require students to explain their reasoning.
choices.tradeoff: Tiered scaffolds give every student a dignified entry point at their level of readiness. The visual representations give Siti access to the concept without the symbolic barrier. But students who are already working abstractly — like Rajan — may find the visual scaffolds unnecessary or constraining. Students who receive the strips may become dependent on the visual and not develop the abstract rule.

choices.label: Accountable Talk: prove equivalence to your partner
choices.elaboration: Assign shoulder partners and ask them to prove their equivalence to each other using the stem: 'These fractions are equivalent because ___. I know this because ___.' The listener must ask: 'Can you prove it a different way?' Circulate and listen for the most common reasoning patterns and errors. After 3 minutes, select two contrasting pairs — one with a clear proof, one with an error or shallow claim — and run a brief IRF probe: 'What did you notice about these two proofs? Which reasoning is stronger? Why?'
choices.tradeoff: Accountable Talk makes the reasoning visible through talk and gives students a low-stakes way to articulate their understanding. The IRF probe allows you to surface and correct errors publicly. But pairs where both students have the same error will reinforce the misconception. Siti, who has not attempted any fractions, has nothing to prove — she may echo her partner without engaging.

A.consequence: The responses come in and the pattern is clear. 15 of 24 students got the correct missing numerator (6). But on the reasoning question, only 10 of 24 chose option (a) — multiply top and bottom by the same number. 5 students chose option (b) — common denominator — which is a different but valid method. 4 students chose option (c) — guessing. 5 students, including Siti, left the reasoning blank. Nur got the answer right but chose option (b) — she is using the common denominator method, not the multiply-both rule. Rajan got both right and can explain clearly: 'I multiplied top and bottom by 2 because 6 × 2 = 12, so I need to multiply 3 × 2 = 6.' Wei Jie got the answer wrong — he wrote 3/12 instead of 6/12 — and chose option (a), but his working shows he only multiplied the numerator. The misconception is explicit: he knows the rule name but is applying it incorrectly. Siti got the answer wrong and left the reasoning blank. The evidence is now precise: the majority error is multiplying only the numerator, and even students who get correct answers are using different methods. 16 minutes remain.

choices.label: Whole-class reteach: model the multiply-both rule
choices.elaboration: Address the multiplier error directly with the whole class. Write two solutions side by side on the board: the correct method (3/6 → multiply top AND bottom by 2 → 6/12) and the common error (3/6 → multiply top by 2 → 6/6, wrong). Expand to show why the error fails: 6/6 = 1, not 1/2. Emphasise: 'When we find an equivalent fraction, we multiply the numerator AND the denominator by the same number. The fraction looks different but represents the same amount.' Ask all students to redo the diagnostic question using the correct method and write one sentence explaining why both top and bottom must be multiplied.
choices.tradeoff: The reteach addresses the specific error precisely and efficiently. It will reach the students who are multiplying only the numerator and those who are guessing. But it reteaches students like Rajan who already understand, which under-challenges them. Siti may watch and copy the board without building genuine understanding of why both must be multiplied.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) peer feedback on multiplier steps
choices.elaboration: Use the diagnostic evidence to form pairs: students who got both questions right (like Rajan) are paired with students who made the multiplier error (like Wei Jie) or left the reasoning blank (like Siti). The correct student gives AQS feedback on their partner's working: Affirm what is right ('You multiplied the numerator correctly'), Question the error ('Why did you only multiply the top? What happened to the denominator?'), Suggest the fix ('Try multiplying both top and bottom by the same number — the fraction changes its look but not its value'). Circulate and listen for the quality of explanations. After 4 minutes, ask one pair to share their AQS exchange.
choices.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise in the room. Wei Jie, who has a clear patterned error, may benefit from having it questioned by a peer. But Siti has no working to give feedback on — her partner will end up explaining the concept from scratch. Rajan is giving rather than receiving, which may not challenge him.

B.consequence: The scaffold cards go out and students begin working. Rajan receives the abstract extension card, reads it quickly, and writes two proofs: the multiply-both method and a simplification method. He then writes: 'The multiply-both method is more efficient because you can scale up or down in one step.' He has gone beyond the card. Nur receives the number line card and marks both fractions carefully — she can see they land on the same point and writes: 'They are equivalent because they are at the same position on the number line.' But when you ask her 'Can you write the multiply-both rule?', she hesitates and cannot. Wei Jie receives the fraction strips card and works through it slowly. He shades 2/5 and then tries to shade an equivalent — he shades 4/10 correctly using the strips. His working is now visible and correct, but it is slow and dependent on the visual. Siti receives the fraction strips card and copies the shading from the example on the card without engaging with the reasoning. When you ask her 'Are these fractions equivalent?', she says 'Yes' but cannot explain why. The scaffolds have given every student an entry point, but the depth of understanding varies enormously. 17 minutes remain.

choices.label: IRF (Initiate-Response-Feedback) probe: connect visual to abstract rule
choices.elaboration: Pause the class and run a cross-representation IRF chain. Ask: 'Nur, what does your number line tell you about 3/6 and 6/12?' (Initiation — visual). Then ask: 'Wei Jie, can you express what Nur just said using the multiply-both rule?' (Response — abstract). Then ask a third student: 'Can you write a general rule that works for any equivalent fraction?' (Feedback — generalisation). Use the chain to build a complete, class-constructed explanation that moves from visual to abstract. Write the final rule on the board.
choices.tradeoff: The cross-representation IRF chain makes the connection between the visual scaffold and the abstract rule visible and public. It leverages the different strengths in the room and builds a shared explanation. But it puts students on the spot by asking them to work in a mode they may not be confident in. Siti, who is anchored in the visual strips, may freeze if asked to connect to the abstract rule.

choices.label: Scaffold removal: new pair, no visual, self-check
choices.elaboration: Give a new question with no scaffold: '4/5 = ?/20.' Ask students to find the equivalent fraction independently and then write a self-check: 'I multiplied top and bottom by ___. I know this is correct because ___. I check by ___.' Give 3 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has genuinely internalised the rule and who was dependent on the visual scaffold.
choices.tradeoff: Scaffold removal tests genuine internalisation directly. Rajan and Nur are likely ready — Rajan because he was already working abstractly, Nur because she demonstrated understanding on the number line. But Wei Jie, who was working slowly with the strips, may not be ready for abstract work yet. Siti, who was copying the strips without reasoning, will stall immediately. You will have clean evidence — but less time to respond.

C.consequence: The pairs begin talking and the room gets louder. Rajan proves his equivalence to his partner with precision: '3/4 = 9/12 because I multiplied top and bottom by 3. The fraction looks different but represents the same amount — three quarters of anything is the same as nine twelfths of the same thing.' His partner asks: 'Can you prove it a different way?' Rajan draws a diagram. Nur says to her partner: 'They are equivalent because they represent the same amount.' Her partner asks: 'Can you prove it a different way?' Nur says: 'I found the common denominator.' She cannot articulate the multiply-both rule. Wei Jie says to his partner: 'I agree — ×2 makes them equivalent.' His partner nods. Neither student questions whether multiplying only the numerator is correct — the error is being reinforced. Siti echoes her partner's proof word for word without adding anything. You circulate and hear the pattern: 7 pairs have genuine proofs with clear reasoning. 9 pairs are mirroring each other — confirming the same method without questioning it. 8 pairs are shallow or echoing. The talk has revealed some reasoning but has also reinforced errors in the pairs where both students have the same misconception. 16 minutes remain.

choices.label: AQS (Affirm-Question-Suggest) gallery critique on proofs
choices.elaboration: Ask each pair to write their proof on a sticky note in one sentence: 'These fractions are equivalent because ___.' Post four notes on the board: one with a clear multiply-both proof, one with a common-denominator proof, one with the multiplier error ('×2 numerator only'), one with a shallow claim ('same amount'). Run a whole-class AQS session on the error note: Affirm what they attempted ('They identified the correct multiplier'), Question the error ('Why did you only multiply the numerator? What happened to the denominator?'), Suggest the fix ('Multiply both top and bottom by the same number — try it and check'). Ask all pairs to revise their proofs.
choices.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the multiplier error visible and public in a structured, non-embarrassing way. It gives the mirroring pairs a concrete model of what a correct proof looks like. But the pairs whose error note is displayed may feel exposed. Siti's pair will likely produce a shallow claim, and the AQS feedback may feel abstract to students who have not yet attempted the fractions.

choices.label: Solo equivalence proof: write three steps
choices.elaboration: Stop the pair talk and ask all students to work individually. Write on the board: 'Choose one of your equivalent pairs. Write: (1) The equivalent pair is ___ = ___. (2) I found this by ___. (3) I know it is correct because ___.' Give 3 minutes, then collect the responses. Scan them to see who has genuine understanding and who was borrowing their partner's reasoning during the talk.
choices.tradeoff: Solo written proofs give you clean, individual evidence of each student's understanding — uncontaminated by pair talk. Rajan's proof will show deep understanding. But the move to individual work kills the emerging talk momentum. Siti, who was echoing her partner, will likely produce a blank or copied response — revealing that the pair talk did not help her develop her own reasoning.

A1.outcome: You write both methods on the board — the correct multiply-both rule and the common error — and work through why the error fails. The room goes quiet as students watch. Wei Jie looks at the board and immediately sees his error. He erases his working and rewrites: '2/5 = 4/10 — multiply top AND bottom by 2.' He checks: '2 × 2 = 4, 5 × 2 = 10. ✓' He looks relieved. Nur follows the reteach and rewrites her working to show the multiply-both step explicitly. She adds: 'I was using common denominators — this is the same thing but written differently.' Rajan watches the reteach with visible disengagement — he already understood this and the whole-class explanation does not challenge him. He returns to generating his own fraction chains. Siti copies the worked example from the board line by line. When you circulate and ask her 'Can you try the next question using the same method?', she looks at the board, copies the structure, and fills in the numbers. Her answer is correct, but when you ask 'Why do we multiply both top and bottom?', she says 'Because you showed us.' On a follow-up check, 20 of 24 students now show the multiply-both step correctly, up from 15. The reteach has addressed the majority error. But Rajan is under-challenged and Siti is compliant without understanding. 13 minutes remain.
A1.tradeoff: The whole-class reteach with worked example and non-example is efficient and reaches the majority of students who had the multiplier error. It is process-level feedback (Hattie & Timperley) — you addressed the specific procedure that went wrong and explained why the correct method works. Wei Jie and Nur made clear gains. But the reteach treats the class as uniform. Rajan is under-challenged and Siti is copying rather than understanding.
A1.reflection:
Why did you choose whole-class reteach over peer feedback? What were you prioritising — efficiency, accuracy, or equity?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Wei Jie and Nur corrected their errors, but Rajan is disengaged and Siti is copying. What does this tell you about the difference between fixing a procedural error and building conceptual understanding of equivalent fractions?

How do you design feedback that builds the understanding of why both numerator and denominator must be multiplied, rather than just correcting the mechanics?

A2.outcome: You pair Rajan with Wei Jie and ask Rajan to give AQS feedback on Wei Jie's working. Rajan looks at Wei Jie's page and begins: 'You multiplied the numerator correctly — 2 × 2 = 4. But you only multiplied the top. The denominator needs to be multiplied by the same number: 5 × 2 = 10. So 2/5 = 4/10.' Wei Jie listens, nods, and rewrites his working. He checks: '4/10 — yes, that is right.' Rajan then explains why: 'If you only multiply the top, you change the value of the fraction. Multiplying both keeps the ratio the same.' Wei Jie writes this down. Nur is paired with a student who used the common denominator method. Her partner gives AQS feedback: 'Your answer is right, but can you show the multiply-both step? That is what the question is asking for.' Nur rewrites her working to show the multiply step explicitly. Siti is paired with a student who can explain the rule but struggles to articulate the reasoning. Her partner says: 'You multiply top and bottom by the same number.' Siti copies this onto her page but when you ask 'Why?', she says 'Because my partner said so.' On a follow-up check, 18 of 24 students now show the multiply-both step correctly, up from 15. The peer feedback has activated genuine reasoning for Wei Jie and Nur. But Siti is still copying rather than understanding. 13 minutes remain.
A2.tradeoff: AQS peer feedback is more personalised than a whole-class reteach and activates the expertise in the room. Wei Jie and Nur made real gains through structured peer exchange. But Siti's partner could not explain the reasoning behind the rule, so Siti remains dependent on copying. Rajan is giving rather than receiving — explaining is harder than doing, but he is not being challenged by new content.
A2.reflection:
Why did you choose AQS peer feedback over whole-class reteach? What were you hoping would happen?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Wei Jie and Nur made genuine gains, but Siti is still copying and Rajan is under-challenged. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for productive peer feedback in a mixed-readiness class?

How do you design peer support so that both the helper and the helped are genuinely learning? What would Rajan need to make this a real learning experience for him too?

B1.outcome: You pause the class and run the cross-representation IRF chain. You ask Nur: 'What does your number line tell you about 3/6 and 6/12?' Nur looks at her number line and says: 'They are at the same point — so they represent the same amount.' You ask Wei Jie: 'Can you express what Nur just said using the multiply-both rule?' Wei Jie pauses, then says: 'If they are at the same point, it means we multiplied top and bottom by the same number — 3/6 × 2/2 = 6/12.' You write this on the board. You ask a third student: 'Can you write a general rule that works for any equivalent fraction?' The student says: 'Multiply numerator and denominator by the same number — the fraction changes its appearance but not its value.' The IRF chain produces a complete, class-constructed rule. Rajan listens with interest — the chain has articulated the rule more precisely than he had in his own notes. Siti watches but does not contribute. When you ask her 'Does that make sense?', she nods quickly but does not speak. Her page still shows only copied shading from the strips card. On a follow-up check, 17 of 24 students can now articulate the multiply-both rule, up from 10. 13 minutes remain.
B1.tradeoff: The cross-representation IRF chain makes the connection between the visual scaffold and the abstract rule visible and public. It leverages the different strengths in the room and builds a shared explanation that is richer than any single representation. Rajan was unexpectedly engaged by the precision of the class-built rule. But Siti did not contribute and the chain did not give her an entry point into the abstract rule. The probe served the middle group well but did not stretch Rajan or support Siti.
B1.reflection:
Why did you choose to run a cross-representation IRF chain rather than removing the scaffold immediately? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? The chain produced strong reasoning from the middle group, but Siti remained passive. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for whole-class probing to include all students?

How do you use visual scaffolds in a way that builds toward the abstract rule, rather than becoming a permanent crutch?

B2.outcome: You give the new question — 4/5 = ?/20 — with no scaffold. Rajan completes it in under a minute with clear working: '4/5 × 4/4 = 16/20. I multiplied top and bottom by 4 because 5 × 4 = 20.' His self-check says: 'I know this is correct because 4/5 and 16/20 are at the same point on a number line. I check by simplifying 16/20 ÷ 4/4 = 4/5 ✓.' His response shows deep understanding and metacognitive awareness. Nur attempts the question and gets it right: '4/5 = 16/20 — multiply top and bottom by 4.' Her self-check says: 'I know because 5 × 4 = 20, so I multiply 4 × 4 = 16. I check by dividing back.' She has internalised the rule from the number line work. Wei Jie writes '4/5 = 8/20' — he has multiplied only the numerator by 2 instead of both by 4. His self-check says: 'I multiplied by 2. I check by... I am not sure.' The scaffold removal has revealed that he was dependent on the visual strips and has not yet internalised the abstract rule. Siti writes '4/5 = ?' and stops. Her self-check is blank. The transfer task has revealed a clear split: Rajan and Nur have internalised the rule; Wei Jie and Siti have not. 13 minutes remain.
B2.tradeoff: Scaffold removal tests genuine internalisation honestly. Rajan and Nur demonstrated real understanding — they could execute the rule and explain the reasoning. But Wei Jie and Siti stalled when the visual support was removed. Wei Jie's error reveals he was using the strips to get correct answers without internalising the multiply-both rule. Siti has produced no new independent work. You have clean evidence of the gap — but limited time to address it.
B2.reflection:
Why did you choose to remove the scaffold and test transfer immediately? What were you hoping to learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur succeeded, but Wei Jie reverted to his error and Siti stalled completely. What does this tell you about the timing of scaffold removal?

How do you know when a student is ready to work without a visual scaffold? What evidence would tell you it is too soon — and what would you do differently next time?

C1.outcome: Four sticky notes are posted on the board. You run the AQS session on the error note: '×2 numerator only.' Several students affirm: 'They identified the correct multiplier.' You write this on the board. Then you ask: 'What should we question?' Nur raises her hand: 'They only multiplied the top. The denominator needs to be multiplied by the same number.' You write: 'Question: Why only the numerator? What happens to the denominator?' A third student suggests: 'Multiply both top and bottom by 2 — then check that the fraction simplifies back to the original.' You write: 'Suggest: Multiply both. Check by simplifying.' The AQS feedback is now complete and visible. You ask all pairs to revise their proofs. Wei Jie looks at the board and rewrites his working: '2/5 = 4/10 — multiply top AND bottom by 2.' He checks and confirms it is correct. Nur revises her proof to show the multiply-both step explicitly. Siti's pair produces a revised note that says 'multiply both' but when you ask Siti to demonstrate on a new question, she hesitates and writes the wrong answer. The AQS process has corrected the error for most students, but Siti has copied the correction without understanding it. 13 minutes remain.
C1.tradeoff: The AQS gallery critique makes the multiplier error visible and correctable in a structured, public way. It gave the mirroring pairs a concrete model of what a correct proof looks like, and Wei Jie self-corrected after seeing the feedback. But Siti copied the correction without understanding — the AQS process served the middle group but did not give her a genuine entry point into the reasoning.
C1.reflection:
Why did you choose to use the gallery talk output as the basis for AQS feedback? What were you hoping the class would learn?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Wei Jie self-corrected and Nur deepened her proof, but Siti copied the correction without understanding. What does this tell you about the conditions needed for AQS feedback to activate genuine reasoning rather than just correct copying?

How do you design pair tasks so that students prove equivalence genuinely rather than confirm each other's errors?

C2.outcome: The room goes quiet as students write their three-step proofs individually. Rajan writes a detailed proof: '(1) 3/4 = 9/12. (2) I found this by multiplying top and bottom by 3: 3 × 3 = 9, 4 × 3 = 12. (3) I know it is correct because 9/12 simplifies back to 3/4 when I divide both by 3.' His proof is complete, precise, and shows metacognitive awareness. Nur writes: '(1) 3/6 = 6/12. (2) I multiplied top and bottom by 2. (3) I know because 6/12 ÷ 2/2 = 3/6.' Her proof shows genuine understanding of the rule. Wei Jie writes: '(1) 2/5 = 4/10. (2) I multiplied by 2. (3) I check by... I am not sure.' His proof reveals he still has not articulated why both must be multiplied — he knows the answer is right but cannot explain the reasoning. Siti writes: '(1) 2/5 = ?' and stops. Her proof is blank beyond the first fraction. When you circulate and ask her 'Can you try step 2?', she shakes her head. The solo proofs have given you honest individual evidence. Rajan and Nur have genuine understanding; Wei Jie has the procedure but not the reasoning; Siti has not engaged with the task. 13 minutes remain.
C2.tradeoff: Solo written proofs give you the clearest, most honest picture of each student's understanding — uncontaminated by pair talk. Rajan and Nur demonstrated genuine understanding with complete, self-checking proofs. But the move to individual work has stopped the emerging talk. Wei Jie's proof reveals he has the procedure but not the conceptual reasoning. Siti's blank proof reveals that the pair talk gave her nothing to work with independently.
C2.reflection:
Why did you choose to move from pair talk to solo written proofs? What were you trying to find out?

Are you satisfied with the outcome? Rajan and Nur showed genuine understanding, but Wei Jie has the procedure without the reasoning and Siti has nothing. What does this tell you about when pair talk builds genuine understanding versus borrowed answers?

What does it mean to understand equivalent fractions rather than just produce them? How do you design tasks that make that understanding visible from the start of the lesson?
